questions.question_text: A California buyer's agent represents only the buyer throughout a transaction. Which agency relationship does this describe?
distractors: Dual agent
Seller's subagent
Transaction coordinator
questions.explanation: A buyer's agent represents solely the buyer's interests. California's agency-disclosure law (Civil Code §§2079.13–2079.24) effectively ended subagency in 1-4 unit residential sales, so cooperating agents today work as direct buyer's agents rather than subagents of the listing broker.

questions.question_text: Under California's residential agency-disclosure law (Civil Code §§2079.13–2079.24), which form of representation is no longer recognized in 1-4 unit transactions?
distractors: Dual agency
Buyer's agency
Non-agent transaction coordination
questions.explanation: Civil Code §§2079.13–2079.24 require explicit disclosure of which party (or parties) a real estate licensee represents in a 1-4 unit residential transaction. Cooperating brokers in those transactions act as direct buyer's agents, not as subagents of the listing broker.

questions.question_text: A single broker represents both the seller and the buyer in the same transaction. What agency relationship exists?
distractors: Seller's agency with incidental buyer assistance
Non-agent transaction coordination
Designated agency with no conflict
questions.explanation: When one broker (or one brokerage) represents both buyer and seller in the same transaction, that broker is a dual agent. In California, dual agency is legal only with written consent from both parties.

questions.question_text: An agent facilitates a transaction without representing either party's interests, handling paperwork and coordination only. What best describes this role in California?
distractors: Seller's agent acting in a limited capacity
Dual agent with reduced fiduciary duties
Broker associate acting independently
questions.explanation: California recognizes a non-agent or transaction coordinator role in which the licensee facilitates the transaction without representing either party. This person does not owe full fiduciary duties to either side.

questions.question_text: A California buyer's agent has been showing homes to a client for three weeks without signing any representation agreement. Which best describes the risk of this arrangement?
questions.correct_answer: The agency relationship and its protections may be unclear or disputed
distractors: The agent automatically becomes a dual agent after 30 days
The agent is presumed to be a seller's subagent under state law
The buyer forfeits the right to an agency disclosure form
questions.explanation: Without a written agreement, the agency relationship and each party's duties are undefined. California's agency statutes contemplate clear written disclosure and confirmation; an undocumented relationship creates legal ambiguity that can harm both agent and client.

questions.question_text: For which transactions is a California Agency Disclosure (AD) form required?
questions.correct_answer: Sales, exchanges, or leases over one year for 1-4 unit residential properties
distractors: All residential and commercial real estate sales
Residential sales only, regardless of lease term or unit count
Any transaction where a broker represents both parties
questions.explanation: Under Civil Code §2079 et seq., the AD form is required for sales, exchanges, or leases of more than one year involving 1-4 unit residential properties. Commercial and multi-unit transactions have different rules.

questions.question_text: A listing agent meets with a seller to sign a listing agreement. When must the agent provide the Agency Disclosure form?
questions.correct_answer: At or before the time the listing agreement is signed
distractors: Before the first offer is presented to the seller
Within three days of the seller accepting an offer
At closing, included with the settlement statement
questions.explanation: California law requires the listing agent to provide the Agency Disclosure form to the seller before — or at the same time as — the listing agreement is signed. Earlier is better; it cannot be provided after the listing is executed.

questions.question_text: A buyer's agent shows homes to a buyer and is about to submit an offer. When must the buyer receive the Agency Disclosure form?
questions.correct_answer: Before the buyer signs any offer to purchase
distractors: Before closing escrow on any accepted offer
Within 24 hours of the offer being presented to the seller
At the same time as the buyer's inspection report
questions.explanation: The buyer's agent must provide the Agency Disclosure form to the buyer before the buyer signs an offer to purchase. The law requires this disclosure as early as practicable but no later than the moment the buyer commits to an offer.

questions.question_text: Where must the agency confirmation appear in a California residential transaction?
questions.correct_answer: In the purchase agreement itself, as required by CC §2079.17
distractors: In a separate standalone document signed by both parties
In the escrow instructions prepared by the escrow officer
In the listing agreement, acknowledged by both buyer and seller
questions.explanation: Civil Code §2079.17 requires agency confirmation to appear within the purchase agreement. This ensures both buyer and seller formally acknowledge each agent's role in the specific transaction.

questions.question_text: A listing agent signs a purchase agreement with the buyer but forgets to include the agency confirmation section. What is the consequence?
questions.correct_answer: The agent may face disciplinary action and the omission may create agency disputes
distractors: The purchase agreement is void as a matter of law
The transaction proceeds normally; confirmation is optional in residential sales
The escrow officer must add the confirmation before closing
questions.explanation: Failure to include agency confirmation in the purchase agreement violates CC §2079.17. While the contract itself may not be automatically void, the agent risks discipline and the omission can create disputes about representation roles.

questions.question_text: A California dual agent is asked by the seller to reveal that the buyer would pay $20,000 more than the listed price. What must the dual agent do?
questions.correct_answer: Decline to disclose the buyer's confidential pricing information
distractors: Disclose the buyer's maximum price to help the seller negotiate
Withdraw from dual agency and represent the seller only
Obtain written consent from both parties before disclosing
questions.explanation: A dual agent may not disclose one party's confidential financial information — including the buyer's willingness to pay more — to the other party. This is a key limitation of dual agency that both parties must understand.

questions.question_text: A buyer and seller each independently agree in writing to allow their shared broker to act as a dual agent. Is this arrangement valid in California?
questions.correct_answer: Yes, dual agency is legal with written consent from both parties
distractors: No, dual agency is prohibited in California regardless of consent
Yes, but only if each party is represented by a separate salesperson in the brokerage
No, unless the broker waives all commission to eliminate the conflict
questions.explanation: Dual agency is legal in California provided the broker obtains written, informed consent from both the buyer and the seller. Without this dual consent, acting as a dual agent violates the licensee's fiduciary duties.

questions.question_text: In a California transaction, which party determines the commission amount paid to the listing broker?
questions.correct_answer: The seller and listing broker negotiate the commission freely
distractors: The local MLS sets standard commission rates
The California Association of Realtors publishes approved rates
The DRE establishes maximum allowable commission percentages
questions.explanation: Real estate commissions are always negotiable in California. The seller and listing broker agree on the commission amount through their listing agreement. No standard rate is mandated by the MLS, trade associations, or the DRE.

questions.question_text: A salesperson closes a deal and the buyer wants to pay her a bonus directly. May she accept it?
questions.correct_answer: No, compensation must be paid through the employing broker only
distractors: Yes, if the bonus is disclosed in writing to both parties
Yes, bonuses under $1,000 do not require broker involvement
No, unless the DRE approves the direct payment arrangement
questions.explanation: California law requires that all compensation to a salesperson flow through the employing broker. A salesperson cannot accept direct payment from a buyer, seller, or any other party outside this channel.

questions.question_text: Two cooperating brokers dispute which one is entitled to a commission because both claim to have introduced the buyer to the property. Which legal doctrine resolves this dispute?
distractors: First-in-time priority rule for license date
Exclusive right of sale provision in the listing
Dual agency commission splitting requirement
questions.explanation: The procuring cause doctrine determines which broker's efforts were the direct and primary cause of the completed sale. When two agents both claim credit, procuring cause analysis (often conducted by the MLS or through arbitration) resolves the dispute.
